macro_segments.title: Review of Principles of Psak from Previous Daf: When Halacha Follows the Lenient Opinion

micro_segments.title: Introduction to Review: Following the Lenient Position in Rabbinic Matters and the Exception of Eruvin and Mourning
Relying on Minority Opinions in Times of Pressing Need (Sha'at Hadeḥak) and the Authority of the Shulchan Aruch
Rules for Following Tannaitic Disputes: When Halacha Follows Rabbi Yehuda, Rabbi Meir, Rabbi Akiva, and Rabbi Yosi
Rav Mesharshya's Challenge to the General Principles and the Gemara's Response: Principles Apply Only When No Explicit Ruling Exists

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Method of Proving and Testing the Principles Through Cases of Exceptions and Cases Requiring Explicit Rulings

micro_segments.title: Cases Where the Gemara Rules Against the Principles and the Answer of Exceptions to the Rules
Cases Where the Principles Hold But Require Explicit Ruling, Raising the Question of Why the Principle Needs Stating

macro_segments.title: Case One: A Private City Becoming Public and Vice Versa, and How to Make an Eruv

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah's Discussion of Reshut Harabbim and Whether Ownership or Usage Determines Public Domain Status
The Case of a City Transitioning from Private to Public and the Requirement to Leave Part of the City Without Eruv as a Memorial
Debate Between Rabbi Yehuda and Rabbi Shimon Regarding the Size of Area to Exclude
Rav Chama bar Guria Reporting Rav's Ruling That the Halacha Follows Rabbi Shimon

macro_segments.title: Case Two: When a Householder Leaves His House to Spend Shabbat Elsewhere, Whether He Prevents the Neighbors from Making an Eruv

micro_segments.title: The Principle That All Members of a Courtyard Must Participate in the Eruv
The Mishnah's Discussion of Whether an Absent Person (Jew or Non-Jew) Prevents the Eruv
Rabbi Meir's Position: Both Jew and Non-Jew Who Leave Prevent the Eruv
Rabbi Yehuda's Position: Neither Jew Nor Non-Jew Prevent the Eruv
Rabbi Yosi's Position: Non-Jew Prevents the Eruv Because He Can Travel on Shabbat; Jew Does Not If He Left Willingly
Rav Chama bar Guria Reports Rav's Ruling in Favor of Rabbi Shimon's Distinctions and Challenge from the Principles

macro_segments.title: Case Three: Using One's Own Physical Presence Instead of Bread for Eruv Techumin

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah's Case of a Poor Person Making an Eruv Techumin With His Legs Instead of Bread
Rabbi Meir's Restriction: Only a Poor Person Can Use Physical Presence
Rabbi Yehuda's Position: Anyone Can Use Physical Presence; Bread Was a Leniency for the Rich
Rav's Explicit Teaching to Rabbi Chiya Bar Ashi That the Halacha Follows Rabbi Yehuda
The Gemara's Challenge: Why State the Explicit Ruling If the Principles Already Determine the Outcome?

macro_segments.title: Resolution: Distinction Between Mishnayot and Baraitot, and Discussion of Shmuel's Principle on Rabbi Meir's Gezeirot

micro_segments.title: Alternative Explanation: Rav Does Not Hold of These General Principles, Only Rabbi Yochanan Does

macro_segments.title: Case Four: Marriage and Betrothal Within Three Months of Widowhood or Divorce

micro_segments.title: The Halakhic Concern About Paternity and the Three-Month Waiting Period Before a Woman Can Remarry
The Mishnah's Blanket Rule: All Women Must Wait Three Months Before Nisuin or Eirusin
Rabbi Yehuda's Logical Distinctions: Conditions Under Which Eirusin or Nisuin Are Permitted Before Three Months
Rabbi Yosi's Position: All Women Can Have Eirusin Immediately Except Widows in Aveilus
Rabbi Yochanan's Explicit Ruling That the Halacha Follows Rabbi Yosi
Challenge: Why State the Explicit Ruling If the Principles of Following Rabbi Yosi Against Rabbi Meir Already Hold?

macro_segments.title: Resolution: Distinction Between Mishnayot and Baraitot, and Discussion of Shmuel's Principle on Rabbi Meir's Gezeirot (continued)

micro_segments.title: Abaye's Explanation: The Principles May Not Apply to Baraitot With the Same Certainty as Mishnayot
Shmuel's Principle: The Halacha Follows Rabbi Meir in His Gezeirot (Stringent Decrees)
Conclusion: Rabbi Yochanan's Explicit Ruling Contradicts Shmuel's Principle, Necessitating the Explicit Statement

macro_segments.title: Case Five: Purchasing Items at Pagan Markets to Bring Them Under Jewish Ownership

micro_segments.title: The Baraita Permitting Purchase of Servants, Animals, and Land in Eretz Yisrael at Non-Jewish Marketdays
Special Allowance for a Kohen to Travel to Chutz Laaretz to Defend Land Purchases in Non-Jewish Courts
Allowance for a Kohen to Become Tameh in Chutz Laaretz to Get Married or Learn Torah
Rabbi Yehuda's Restriction: Only When No Good Yeshiva Exists in Eretz Yisrael
Rabbi Yosi's Disagreement: A Kohen Can Go to Chutz Laaretz Even With Good Yeshivot in Eretz Yisrael
Rabbi Yochanan's Explicit Ruling Following Rabbi Yosi, and Abaye's Explanation About Baraitot

macro_segments.title: Discussion of Objects Owned by Non-Jews and Their Techumin Status

micro_segments.title: Shmuel's Position: Objects Owned by Non-Jews Do Not Acquire a Fixed Place (Do Not Have Techumin)
Reasoning: Non-Jewish Objects Are Defined by Their Owner's Techumin, and Non-Jews Have No Techumin Limit
Baraita Contradicting Shmuel: Objects Owned by Non-Jews at the Start of Yom Tov Have Techumin of 2000 Amot
Gemara's Analysis: The Baraita Assumes Rabbi Yochanan ben Nuri's Position or Rav Chiya bar Avin's Position That Non-Jewish Objects Have Techumin
Rav Chiya bar Avin Reports Rabbi Yochanan's Position: Non-Jewish Objects Have Techumin Due to Concern About Confusion With Jewish-Owned Objects

macro_segments.title: Case Six: Rams Brought Outside the Techumin and Sold in the City of Mavrakhta

micro_segments.title: Rava Allows the People of Mavrakhta to Purchase Rams That Were Brought Outside the Techumin
Ravina's Challenge: How Can Rava Allow This Given Shmuel's Position on Non-Jewish Objects?
Rava's Resolution: The Entire City of Mavrakhta Is Treated Like Four Amot Because the Objects Entered a Walled City

macro_segments.title: Case Seven: A Fishnet Dividing Waters Between the Techumin of Two Cities

micro_segments.title: The Hypothetical of a Fishnet Marking the Boundary Between Two Cities' Techumin in a Body of Water
Rabbi Chiya's Position: An Iron Wall Is Needed to Divide the Waters and Prevent Mixture
Rabbi Yosi bar Chanina's Objection: Why Laugh at a Stringent Position When We Generally Follow the Lenient Opinion in Eruvin?
Gemara's Explanation: Rabbinic Leniency Regarding Barriers in Water Permits Even Imperfect Divisions

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Yosi m'chavero (Rabbi Yosi against his peer)
Heicha d'itmar itmar, heicha d'lo itmar lo itmar (when said explicitly it was said, when not said it wasn't)
